title: What 'Gentle Parenting' Gets Wrong About Anger
dek: *The movement promised liberation from our parents' rage, but somewhere along the way it started pathologizing the very emotion that signals our boundaries are being violated. What if the problem isn't that you get angry at your kids, but that you've been taught anger itself is a parenting failure?*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Gentle parenting often misframes anger as something to eliminate rather than metabolize, leaving parents emotionally constipated. Children learn more from watching us move through anger with integrity than from our desperate performance of calm. The goal isn't to never feel fury - it's to stop abandoning yourself when you do.

Have you ever stood in your kitchen, fists clenched, jaw tight, telling yourself you're a terrible parent because you wanted to scream? The gentle parenting scripts flood your social feeds. Use your calm voice. Get down on their level. Name their feelings. All useful tools, genuinely, but notice what happens in your body when your four-year-old dumps an entire jar of honey on the dog for the third time this week and you're supposed to chirp, "I see you're experimenting with textures." The rage doesn't disappear because you didn't voice it. It goes somewhere. Usually into your shoulders, your jaw, the back of your skull. Or it leaks out sideways an hour later when your partner asks what's for dinner. In our experience, most parents drawn to gentle frameworks are actually trauma survivors trying not to repeat patterns. That's sacred work. But the cultural interpretation of "gentle" has curdled into something that treats parental anger like a contaminant rather than information. ## The Suppression Trap Gentle Culture Built The core teaching of most gentle parenting literature is sound: children's nervous systems co-regulate with ours, and our dysregulation becomes their dysregulation. True enough. But the application has become a game of emotional whack-a-mole where anger is the mole and you're forever swinging. Psychologist Gabor Maté points out that when we consistently suppress authentic emotional responses to preserve attachment, we fragment ourselves. The child learns that love is conditional on the parent's emotional comfort, which is precisely the dynamic many gentle parents are trying to escape. Your child doesn't need you to be a Buddha. They need you to be a whole person who can feel the full spectrum and not collapse. Robert Augustus Masters distinguishes between anger and aggression in his work on emotional literacy. Anger is clean information: a boundary has been crossed, a need is unmet, something matters and is being threatened. Aggression is what happens when anger isn't allowed to move, when it gets dammed up and then explodes or corrodes from within. Gentle parenting culture, in its Instagram-friendly form, has conflated the two. It treats all parental anger as aggression, as violence-in-waiting. But here's what actually happens in bodies. PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load shows that chronic suppression of anger creates measurable physiological stress. Your blood pressure doesn't care that you used a gentle voice. It registers the cortisol spike from the original trigger plus the secondary spike from suppressing your response. You're running a double metabolic load while performing serenity. ## What Your Anger Actually Knows Anger in parents often carries extremely accurate data. It knows when your time is being disrespected. It knows when a power struggle has stopped being about the shoes and started being about your child testing whether you still exist as a separate person with needs. It knows when you've given 47 things today and have nothing left. The gentle script says: take a deep breath, remember they're not giving you a hard time, they're having a hard time. Sometimes true. But sometimes your kid is absolutely giving you a hard time because they're seven and they're pushing to see if the structure holds. And your anger is the appropriate response to that push. It's the boundary saying, "I'm here, I'm solid, you can press against me and I won't crumble or rage, but I also won't disappear." Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger is like a crying baby. You don't throw the baby out. You pick it up, you hold it, you investigate what it needs. This is radically different from the gentle parenting directive to simply not be angry. The spiritual work isn't bypassing the anger. It's developing enough capacity to be with it without either suppressing it or weaponizing it. Think about the last time you felt real anger at your child. Not irritation, not frustration, but that full-body heat. What was underneath it? Chances are it was something like: my autonomy is gone, my body has been touched without consent for twelve hours straight, I haven't had a thought finish in three days, I need this one thing to go smoothly and it's not. All legitimate. All worthy of acknowledgment. Your anger is trying to protect something valuable. ## The Performance of Perpetual Regulation Scroll through gentle parenting content and you'll see a recurring aesthetic: the mother's face is serene, her voice is modulated, her child is mid-tantrum and she's narrating feelings like a nature documentary host. It's performance art. And it's teaching children that real feelings are something adults have transcended. Over the years I've seen what happens in families where the parent successfully suppresses all visible anger: the children become either parentified emotional managers or they escalate their behavior to astronomical levels trying to get a genuine reaction. They're looking for the edge, the real, the human. A parent who can say, "I'm really angry right now, I need to step into the other room for two minutes" is giving their child something more valuable than scripts. They're showing that big feelings can be held without catastrophe. Your nervous system is not a metronome. It's a living response system, and when you try to flatline it into constant calm, you're working against your own biology. Some herbs can support the body's natural ability to process stress, products like [Organic India Brahmi Gotu Kola](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0041V8F3S?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20), but they're not sedatives for your humanity. They support resilience, not suppression. The performance of perpetual regulation is exhausting because it's a lie. Children are exquisitely tuned lie detectors. They feel the mismatch between your words and your body. They feel the tremor in your calm voice. And they learn that feelings are dangerous, that authenticity is a problem, that love requires constant self-abandonment. ## Repair Is the Real Skill Here's what actually builds secure attachment: rupture and repair. Not the absence of rupture. The gentle parenting ideal of never rupturing is developmentally unnecessary and personally destructive. You will lose your temper. You will say something in a tone you regret. You will slam a door. The question is what happens next. Repair looks like coming back an hour later and saying, "I was really angry earlier and I raised my voice. That wasn't about you being bad. I was overwhelmed and I didn't handle it well. I'm working on that." Not "I'm sorry you felt scared" (which centers their reaction), but "I'm sorry I yelled" (which takes responsibility for your action). Then, crucially, you move on. You don't grovel. You don't over-explain. You model that mistakes are survivable and repairable. In my own practice, the parents who report the most connection with their kids are not the ones who never get angry. They're the ones who get angry, acknowledge it, take space when needed, and come back. Their children learn that relationships can hold conflict. That anger doesn't equal abandonment. That repair is possible. This is where [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) we've developed can be useful, not as a way to eliminate anger but as a way to move it through your system with more awareness. The practices there aren't about becoming a different person. They're about staying present to yourself while the heat moves. ## The Somatic Reality of Suppressed Rage Let's get specific about what happens in tissue. When you feel anger rising and you clamp down on it, your jaw tightens. Your shoulders lift toward your ears. Your breath becomes shallow. Your pelvic floor often grips. Your digestive system slows. This isn't metaphorical. This is measurable muscle tension. Do this hundreds of times and you develop chronic holding patterns. The tension becomes your new baseline. You might not even register it as anger anymore. It shows up as neck pain, as TMJ, as digestive issues, as that weird ache in your mid-back that no amount of stretching touches. Your body is storing what your voice won't say. I worked with a mother once who came in complaining of migraines. Three kids under six, gentle parenting devotee, couldn't understand why her head was splitting open several times a week. We started tracking: every migraine followed a day of swallowed anger. Her body was trying to blow the pressure valve because she wouldn't let it out any other way. When she started allowing herself to feel and voice her anger appropriately, the migraines decreased by seventy percent within two months. Some people find that scent can help create a momentary pause before the clampdown happens. Something like [Plant Therapy Organic Lavender (30 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00LABX3DY?tag=spankyspinola-20) on your wrists or chest can signal your nervous system that you're creating a space for sensation without necessarily acting on it. It's not about calming the anger away. It's about giving yourself a breath of space to feel what's true. ## A Practice: The Anger Inventory Here's something you can do in under five minutes, ideally in the end when the kids are in bed. Sit down with a piece of paper. Write at the top: "Today I felt angry when..." Then list everything, no matter how small. Your toddler refused shoes. Your partner didn't empty the dishwasher. Your mother-in-law texted unsolicited advice. The grocery store was out of the one thing you needed. Don't analyze yet. Just list. You'll probably fill half a page or more. This alone is revelatory. You've been carrying all of this while performing gentle. Now go back through the list and next to each item, write one word for what was underneath the anger. Disrespected. Unseen. Exhausted. Trapped. Dismissed. Touched-out. You'll start to see patterns. Your anger isn't random. It's responding to specific recurring violations of your needs. Finally, pick one item from the list and ask: what would honoring this anger have looked like? Not acting it out, not suppressing it, but honoring it. Maybe it would have looked like saying out loud, "I'm angry right now." Maybe it would have looked like stepping outside for three minutes. Maybe it would have looked like texting a friend, "I want to throw this oatmeal against the wall." The point is to start building a vocabulary of response that isn't binary between suppression and explosion. ## What Children Actually Learn from Your Anger Children in homes where anger is suppressed don't learn that anger doesn't exist. They learn that anger is so dangerous it must be hidden. They learn that their parent's composure is fragile and must be protected. They learn to monitor adult emotions and modulate their own behavior to keep the peace. This is the definition of parentification. Children in homes where anger is expressed destructively learn that anger is violence. That feelings justify harm. That when someone is upset, you're in danger. This is the trauma many gentle parents are trying to prevent. Rightfully so. But children in homes where anger is acknowledged, felt, and moved through with integrity learn something else entirely. They learn that all feelings are permissible. That anger can be expressed without destruction. That adults can be upset and still be safe. That you can be angry at someone and still love them. That feelings pass. Your child watching you say, "I'm so angry I need to go scream into a pillow for a minute" learns more about emotional regulation than a thousand gentle scripts. They see the feeling, they see the choice, they see the action, they see you come back. That's the full cycle. That's what builds resilience. Across the articles we've published on this site, the theme that emerges again and again is that transformation isn't about becoming someone who doesn't feel difficult things. It's about building capacity to feel them without fracturing. For more on this lineage of work, you can explore what we're building at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus is on sustainable spiritual practice that doesn't require you to abandon your humanity. ## The Herbs Won't Fix What Needs Feeling Let's be clear about something: [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) is full of plants that support nervous system resilience, adrenal function, and stress response. They're real. They work. But they're not a bypass around the emotional work of actually letting yourself feel angry. Adaptogens can help your body handle stress more efficiently. Nervines can take the sharp edge off anxiety. But if you're using them to avoid ever feeling your anger, you're just building a more sophisticated suppression mechanism. The goal is not to eliminate your capacity for anger. The goal is to increase your capacity to be with anger without it running you or you running from it. Plants are allies in building resilience, not replacements for authenticity. You can take all the holy basil and ashwagandha in the world, and if you're still swallowing your rage three times a day, your body will eventually present you with the bill. The herbs support the work. They don't do it for you. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### But won't my anger scare my child? Your suppressed anger, leaking out as resentment, coldness, or sudden explosions, is more frightening than your acknowledged anger. Children can handle, "Mommy is very angry right now and needs a minute," much better than they can handle the cognitive dissonance of your words saying one thing while your body radiates fury. What scares children is unpredictability and the sense that feelings are so dangerous they can't be named. ### How do I know if I'm expressing anger appropriately or just being abusive? Appropriate anger is boundaried and doesn't attack personhood. "I'm angry that you drew on the wall" is different from "You're so destructive, what's wrong with you?" Appropriate anger allows the other person dignity. It doesn't shame, threaten abandonment, or use physical intimidation. If you're questioning whether your expression crossed a line, that self-reflection is already evidence you're in different territory than abuse, which typically comes with justification rather than questioning. ### What if I grew up with rage and I'm terrified of becoming my parent? This is the core wound many gentle parents carry. The fear is real and the vigilance makes sense. But here's the thing: your parent likely had no practice in feeling anger without acting it out destructively. You're building that capacity. The fact that you're asking this question means you're already different. You can feel anger and choose your response. That's the skill your parent didn't have. You're not doomed to repeat what you didn't heal from, especially when you're actively working to metabolize rather than suppress. ## The Long Game Nobody's Instagramming The truth about parenting is that it will break you open in ways you can't anticipate. Not because you're doing it wrong, but because it's designed to. Every unresolved piece of your own childhood will eventually surface in your parenting. Every place you learned to abandon yourself will get triggered. Gentle parenting became popular partly because it offered a framework that felt like redemption. A way to not pass on the harm. But somewhere in the popularization, it became another way to perform goodness while quietly suffering. Another set of rules to follow perfectly to prove you're not like them. What if the real work is messier than that? What if it's learning to stay present to your anger, your resentment, your rage at the relentlessness of it all, and discovering that you can feel those things and still be a good parent? Not despite the anger, but including it. Not transcending your humanity, but inhabiting it more fully. The children growing up in homes where feelings are real and repair is reliable will have something more valuable than memories of a perfectly calm parent. They'll have a template for being human. For making mistakes and coming back. For feeling everything and not breaking. That's not something you can script. It's something you live, imperfectly, one rupture and repair at a time. And on the days when it feels like too much, when the anger is sharp and the patience is gone, remember: you're not failing gentle parenting. You're succeeding at being real. Your child needs the second one more than the first. For more on the lineage of emotional work we draw from, you can explore [the about page](/about), where the foundations of this practice are laid out more fully. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on what 'gentle parenting' gets wrong about anger, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
